coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in active packaging: Insights into novel functional elements, response strategies and applications for food preservation

coredata.dc:description: Given the substantial food losses incurred throughout the supply chain, which hinder the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goal, there is an urgent need to develop novel active packaging solutions. These solutions should be capable of releasing functional elements on demand and in a controlled manner to effectively reduce food waste. It is also worth exploring the way different packaging responds to different environmental factors, including pH, temperature, light, and humidity etc. Such responsive behaviours can be synergistic, while it should be consistent with actual demands for preservation. The role of novel functional elements is not limited to antimicrobials, but can also regulate other storage environments such as humidity, light, moisture etc., and there is still a lack of systematic updating of such studies. As more functions are integrated into active packaging, some antimicrobial components such as anthocyanins, AIEgens, metal nanoparticles and carbon dots can also incorporate other functions e.g. freshness indication and light response antimicrobial capacity. The concept of multi-response is growing popular, and the sequential release of functional elements to realize secondary functionality deserves attention. This review also addressed the risk, safety assessment, and commercialization visions of novel active packaging, aiming to provide ideas for the development of multifunctional novel food packaging that reduces the dependence on petrochemical-based materials.

Function integration

row.entry: Myricetin exhibited a fast and sensitive turn-on response to superoxide anion with good linearity, and this fluorescence response results from the oxidation of the neighboring hydroxyl group in the B ring.
Crystallization of quercetin promotes ketone emission in a temperature-dependent manner and was highly resistant to photobleaching.
Polyethyleneimine (PEI) induced assembly, and the growth of PEI restricts intramolecular motions, and the intensity of emission increases by about 200-fold.
The addition of Al3+ increased the fluorescence intensity by more than 12-fold and was accompanied by a color change, with good selectivity for Al3+ in the pH 6–8 range.
β-Cyclodextrin enhanced the fluorescence intensity of isorhamnetin while shifting the main emission peak from 542 nm to 557 nm.
In the presence of β-galactosidase, the β-galactoside residues on the C ring of hyperoside were cleaved, releasing quercetin with AIE activity.
TA could spontaneously polymerize to form polytannins with enhanced fluorescence intensity induced by hydroxyl radicals.

The AIE activity of EGCG is caused by spatial conjugation induced by hydrogen bonding between multiple molecules, and the stability was greatly enhanced by cross-linking with 1,6-hexanediamine (HDMA).

It exhibited a wide range of colors from blue to red at different excitation wavelengths, and could detect Fe3+ quickly and effectively. Chitosan aggregated on the surface of Escherichia coli, so it was possible to detect it by the AIE effect.
Aggregates were formed due to the Mie effect, and the fluorescent color in solution changes from dark blue to yellow under UV light (365 nm).
Berberine chloride is an unconventional rotorless AIEgen that exhibits excellent selectivity for lipid droplet imaging at the cellular level.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Stabilization of phage in edible films or coatings.

row.entry: Phage T4 was immobilized on the surface of polycaprolactone film by EDC/NHS cross-linking reaction with a density of 0.29 phage/μm2, which was dramatically higher than that of 0.10 phage/μm2 in physical adsorption, and the phage distribution was more homogeneous in the chemical method.

In whey protein-purulan polysaccharide complex films, there was almost no loss in the first 10 days, with a decrease of about 2 log PFU cm-2 after 30 days of storage and about 3 log PFU cm−2 after 60 days of storage.
After phage doping into sodium alginate film, the loss of them was about 1 log PFU cm−2 in the first 6 weeks and about 3 log PFU cm−2 after 12 weeks.
Incorporation of phage into gelatin membranes resulted in a loss of approximately 0.6 log PFU g−1 after 6 days.
Phages exhibited a 1 log loss in the production of biodegradable plastic (PHBV) coated films and a 2 log loss in the production of PHBV/nanofiber films.
Mixture of Salmonella phage φ135 and Escherichia coli phage vB_EcoS-EC4 (EC4)
Phages doped into sodium alginate films reduced 3 log PFU cm−2.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: n.a. (not available) indicates not applicable in this context.

row.entry: Phenolic hydroxyl groups were protonated under acidic conditions, leading to the breakdown of the outer shell of the complex supramolecular network.
ZIF−8 skeleton decomposed under acidic conditions.
Pectin protonated carboxyl groups showed reduced binding capacity to positively charged calcium carbonate, leading to decomposition of the calcium carbonate skeleton under acidic conditions.
Phenolic hydroxyl protonation caused decomposition of metal-phenol complexes triggering the release of eugenol and Zn2+.
Hydrolysis of imine bonds under acidic conditions.

entry.bold: Response under alkaline conditions (pH increase response)

row.entry: Deprotonation of carboxyl groups and increased hydrophilicity of the surface under alkaline conditions.
In alkaline environments, electrostatic repulsion of deprotonated carboxyl groups expanded the network and allowed faster release of AEP.
As the pH increased, deprotonation of the carboxyl groups of the L100 polymer led to the extension of the polymer chains and the formation of voids that facilitated the release of CEOs.
As pH increased, deprotonation induced a gel-to-sol macroscopic transition of the fiber network formed in PDA-Pep hydrogels.
Ionization of the carboxyl portion of the gellan gum molecule increased intermolecular repulsion between the helices.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: ZIF-8-Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8; AEP-aminoethyl-phloretin; CEO-Cinnamon essential oil; PDA-polydiacetylene.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Given the substantial food losses incurred throughout the supply chain, which hinder the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goal, there is an urgent need to develop novel active packaging solutions. These solutions should be capable of releasing functional elements on demand and in a controlled manner to effectively reduce food waste. It is also worth exploring the way different packaging responds to different environmental factors, including pH, temperature, light, and humidity etc. Such responsive behaviours can be synergistic, while it should be consistent with actual demands for preservation. The role of novel functional elements is not limited to antimicrobials, but can also regulate other storage environments such as humidity, light, moisture etc., and there is still a lack of systematic updating of such studies. As more functions are integrated into active packaging, some antimicrobial components such as anthocyanins, AIEgens, metal nanoparticles and carbon dots can also incorporate other functions e.g. freshness indication and light response antimicrobial capacity. The concept of multi-response is growing popular, and the sequential release of functional elements to realize secondary functionality deserves attention. This review also addressed the risk, safety assessment, and commercialization visions of novel active packaging, aiming to provide ideas for the development of multifunctional novel food packaging that reduces the dependence on petrochemical-based materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid growth of the world's population and the relative volatility of regional economies, accompanied by large-scale health epidemics or frequent regional conflicts, the food crisis remains to be a critical threat to human society (Behnassi & El Haiba, 2022; Righettini & Bordin, 2023). Meanwhile, the explosive development of the Internet has increased the frequency of cross-regional food transportation, thereby raising the requirements for preservation and storage (Al-Dairi et al., 2022). According to a report released by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) in 2021, the global losses of fruits and vegetables could reach up to 45 % of the total production. These losses were not only quantitative, but also impact nutritional quality and sensory attributes in a variety of ways (Panwar et al., 2024). According to Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production), reducing food waste is one of the core indicators. Hence, the development of bio-based and degradable food packaging is essential for the realization of SDG 12. A significant deficiency of traditional plastic packaging is the degradation of large pieces of plastic into small particles (including microplastics and nanoplastics) under the long-term effect of physico-chemical factors, which poses a significant threat to ecosystems and human health (Kumar et al., 2022). Compared to water, microplastics and nanoplastics exhibit a higher affinity for toxic chemicals, acting as carriers to transport them, including antibiotics, heavy metals (e.g. copper, chromium, lead, zinc), polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), organochlorine pesticides (OCPs), etc., which ultimately become enriched in the food chain and will be transferred to the human body (Barletta et al., 2019; Naqash et al., 2020). To mitigate the negative impacts of plastic packaging, various novel preservation methods have been developed, such as electrolytic water treatment (Huang et al., 2023b), low-temperature plasma treatment (Zhou, Rezaeimotlagh, et al., 2022), pulsed light treatment (Hua et al., 2022) and smart active packaging (Alizadeh-Sani et al., 2020; Li et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2022). The concept of active food packaging systems is relatively broad, always involving the absorption and release of specific substances, including but not limited to hygroscopic and oxygen-absorbing packaging (Wan et al., 2024; Zhao et al., 2020), ethylene scavenging packaging (Fan et al., 2022), antimicrobial packaging (Wang et al., 2022), and antioxidant packaging (Chang et al., 2023).

Numerous compounds derived from natural or renewable sources have been utilized in the construction of active food packaging systems, contributing to advancement of the circular economy (Chang et al., 2022). Polysaccharide-based materials generally contain abundant free hydroxyl groups, which can form hydrogen bonds with other substances to form a film. However, their high hydrophilicity, can weaken their strength under high humidity, necessitating the incorporation of hydrophobic substances for reinforcement (Yin et al., 2023). Starch, pectin, sodium alginate, and cellulose (commonly derived in forms like methylcellulose, hydroxypropylmethylcellulose, carboxymethylcellulose, etc.) are more prevalent. Chitosan, enriched in the shells of marine arthropods and insects, is produced by partially removing the acetyl group of the natural polysaccharide chitin. It is noted for its superior film-forming properties, antimicrobial effects, and low toxicity, serving as both a carrier and an antimicrobial agent (Liu et al., 2023; Zhao, Li, et al., 2023). Protein-based materials are mostly whey proteins, alcohol soluble proteins, soybean isolate proteins, etc. These materials have a robust cross-linking ability and can provide a nitrogen source during degradation, despite their poor water-barrier ability (Murrieta-Martínez et al., 2018). The excellent moisture resistance is attributed to the great hydrophobicity of lipid materials. Materials such as oils, fats, waxes are available from plants and animals but may have problems with surface cracking and greasiness (Yin et al., 2023). The above materials form the main basis of the preservation system through mixing and layering, while their activeness and intelligence are mostly determined by the functional elements incorporated.

Controlled release of functional elements is crucial for active packaging, as it is influenced by the microenvironment in which the food is stored, e.g. temperature and humidity, pH, microorganisms, light, specific chemicals. This process requires a precise release on demand at specific locations and time points (Figs. 2 a, 2 b, and 2 c). Premature release leads to wastage of functional elements and may alter the organoleptic properties of the food, belated release fails to achieve the purpose for which the system was designed (Du et al., 2023). In recent years, emerging functional elements have provided diverse possibilities for active packaging. They not only combat potentially harmful microorganisms but also help regulate the storage microenvironment. Therefore, this review aims to provide an overview of the different functional elements commonly found in such active systems, as well as the different environmental factors that induce their excitation ( Fig. 1). In addition, this review also predicts the future development trend of related research, with a vision to contribute to the development of multifunctional food packaging, and to serve as a reference for exploring the high-value utilization of biodegradable resources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Novel functional elements in active packaging
ce:section.ce:para: Functional elements are critical for extending the shelf life of food products, and the regulation of their release rate is essential for maintaining effective antimicrobial capacity or other properties in the system. As shown in Fig. 2 d, Zhao, Li, et al. (2023) summarized the main release mechanisms of antimicrobial agents into six basic modes: diffusion, dissolution, swelling, degradation, erosion, and disintegration. The above modalities normally function together in a coupled manner and should be rationally designed based on the properties of the substrate and the functional elements. The following section lists in detail the dominant functional elements in these active systems, where some of the materials such as polyphenol-metal networks, liposomes, metal-organic frameworks (MOF), etc. are more unique, which may be more useful for releasing other substances, but they are included in this review because they may sever as add-ons to the other functional elements. At the same time, they may be uniquely suited to modulate other properties of the film. Materials of natural origin, represented by cyclodextrins and chitosan, have been extensively reviewed and therefore are not categorized separately.

Natural polyphenols are typical antioxidants, more than 8000 kinds of which have been discovered so far, with properties e.g. UV absorption, metal ion complexation, and are also the mainstream antimicrobial materials currently applied in active packaging systems (Górniak et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2020). Zhao, Guo, et al. (2023) found that the antioxidant activity of natural polypeptide nanofabrics doped with 15 % gallic acid was enhanced by about ten-fold compared to that without polyphenols, but a small amount of dimers or oligomers were produced between the polyphenols, which resulted in a handful of bead-like vesicles in the nanofibers. Kang et al. (2023) incorporated tea polyphenol-loaded pullulan polysaccharide/alginose into film with the assistance of ultrasound, and the optimized sonication strengthened the hydrogen bonding, leading to a more ordered molecular arrangement. Its antimicrobial properties were excellent, with the film exhibiting approximately 100 % lethality against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus at 4 and 8 hours, respectively. Porous microspheres loaded with antimicrobial tannic acid (TA) became the CO2/O2 ratio in lychee packaging and inhibited polyphenol oxidase and guaiacol peroxidase activities thereby reducing oxidative damage (Ma et al., 2021). Apigenin belongs to the family of flavonoids, and the film it produced with biogenic amines showed significant inhibition of Candida albicans (Thirukumaran et al., 2019).

In addition, polyphenols such as TA and tea polyphenols allow coordination with metal ions, providing a viable option for enhancing the intensity of films ( Figs. 3 b and 3 c). Such networks tend to autodissociate under acidic conditions, and can aid in the design of automatic release in response to pH (Xu et al., 2023). In 2013, Ejima et al. (2013) developed a simple multi-application coating through the coordination of TA and Fe3 +, which is considered an essential event as the beginning of phenolic nanotechnology. Phenolic-enabled nanotechnology (PEN) is available in three main forms: phenolic-based nanoparticles, phenolic-based surface coatings, and secondary growth on phenolic-based surfaces (Fig. 3 a), and allows for the design of nanostructures with controllable size, shape, and degradability, as well as surface chemistry potential (Wu et al., 2021). This technology has begun to be applied in the food field in recent years, precisely realizing the controlled release of target substances, biosensing and other functions (Li et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2023).

Conventional luminophores emit brightly in dilute solutions but are very dim or even non-luminous when aggregated, known as aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). Tang's group proposed the concept of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) in 2001, where aggregation-induced emission luminogens (AIEgens) can exhibit high emission properties in the aggregated state (Luo et al., 2001). AIEgens are easy to synthesize, highly customizable, and biocompatible, and are available from renewable resources and have applications in food hazards detection, light-responsive antimicrobials, and so on (Huang et al., 2020b; Lu et al., 2022). Numerous polyphenolic materials, especially flavonols, have advantages in the preparation of AIEgens and exhibit a more secure profile (Tong et al., 2021). Li et al. (2023a) prepared three AIE nanocrystals derived from natural flavonols (populin, quercetin, and kaempferol) and reported the improvement of water vapor barrier, mechanical and thermal properties, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties of the films. Combinations between biomaterials with AIE activity may enhance such property. Berberine, a naturally occurring isoquinoline alkaloid, could self-assemble with flavonoid glycosides to form nanoparticles or nanofibers (Figs. 3 d and 3 e), which performed better inhibitory activity than either one alone (Li et al., 2019). Other non-polyphenolic materials commonly found in food products may have similar effects, such as the presence of AIE properties in nanoparticles formed by the assemble of berberine and cinnamic acid, which can trigger reactive oxygen species (ROS) release under white light (Ma et al., 2022). Materials of biological origin with AIE potential and their main characteristics are listed in Table 1. This strategy changes the tradition of utilizing functional elements to kill bacteria by direct contact and represents a breakthrough for novel active packaging systems.

Metal nanoparticles as to achieve microbial killing through mechanisms such as cation release, generation of ROS, and membrane damage (Slavin et al., 2017). Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) have a broad antimicrobial spectrum. When AgNPs acted in conjunction with sodium alginate/gelatin film, there appeared to be a significant increase in inhibition circle size for Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus (Fig. 3 f). After 15 days of preservation, the Ag content in citrus was 7.49 ng/g, which was well below the safety limit (50 μg/L), indicating that the migration of Ag was within acceptable limits (Li et al., 2022a). AgNPs could also be alloyed with CuNPs to enhance the interfacial catalytic activity, exhibiting excellent antimicrobial activity even at low concentrations, and bio-nanocomposite films with Ag-Cu nanoparticle loadings of 1–4 % showed 0.5–3 and 3.5–7 Log reductions in Listeria monocytogenes and Salmonella typhimurium, respectively (Arfat et al., 2017). Cu2+ has been found to increase intracellular ROS levels in microbial cells, where superoxide (O2 -) is particularly prone to damage DNA and inhibit dehydratases, and also promotes lipid peroxidation in bacteria and yeast, thought to be a major source of their cellular activity (Hong et al., 2012; Macomber et al., 2007). Moreover, stable superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like nanoenzymes based on natural enzyme active sites can be obtained by functionalizing melanin nanoparticles with Cu2+, remarkably improving the antioxidant capacity of the films (Liang et al., 2023).

Metal oxide nanoparticles have numerous applications in food packaging as well, Nikolic et al. (2021) provide a detailed review of its specific development process. When Ag2O particles were deposited on the surface of hemp fibers and modified the polylactic acid (PLA)-nanocellulose film, the samples were found to have great water solubility of less than 1 %. The composite film has favorable vapor permeability and biodegradability compared to plastic film, and the hardness of red grapes barely changes after 15 days of storage under its protection (Liao et al., 2023). Zhu et al. (2023) utilized surfactants to promote the dispersion of nano-ZnO in the film, the vapor produced by transpiration of fruits and vegetables during respiration was not easy to form a fog inside the package, and the film with the best effect had an anti-fog duration of up to 166 hours. Different coating methods (dipping, brushing, spraying, electrostatic spraying) were applied to the surface of mango to form a thin film, and it is found that the electrostatic spraying method has the shortest drying time and nearly the same freshness preservation ability as the traditional brushing method. However, less raw material was consumed and the transfer efficiency was higher (Wang et al., 2023). What's more, TiO2-gelatin nanocomposite coatings encapsulated expanded polyethylene (EPE) foam nets resulted in the photocatalytic degradation of ethylene, and the kinetic data were fitted to the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model to obtain apparent rates, which can be continued to adjust the material parameters. After 96 hours of treatment under UV-A light, coated packages accumulated 60 % less ethylene than controls (Fonseca et al., 2021). As illustrated in Fig. 3 g, Ag2O-TiO2-Bi2WO6 also exhibited photocatalytic degradation of ethylene, which may be attributed to the heterogeneous structure formed among the three oxides to promote the separation and transfer of photocarriers (Xie et al., 2022). However, some safety assessments in recent years have concluded that TiO2 is not safe and that its application requires further discussion (Nikolic et al., 2021).

MOFs are organic-inorganic hybrid materials formed by the self-assembly of metals or metal clusters with organic ligands through coordination bonds, Fig. 3 h elucidates the synthesis of MOF structures and the factors affecting them. The antibacterial mechanisms promoted by MOF nanoparticles are confined to three: microbial uptake, contact with microorganisms, and photodynamic generation of ROS (Nong et al., 2021). UiO-66 is a zirconium-based MOF commonly used in composite film manufacturing that can act as an active agent in smart intelligent packaging. Encapsulating melatonin in the structure formed by UiO-66 and sodium alginate, melatonin then utilized the microporous structure in the MOF to achieve a slow release, regulating the activity of key antioxidant enzymes in plants, making their oxidative stress response more rational (Wang et al., 2024). Not coincidentally, MOF is well suited for loading active substances. Utilizing Ag-MOF loaded with lactoferrin, the film, in addition to disrupting microbial cell membranes, exhibited strong ultraviolet shielding properties, which protects many natural pigments, nutrients, and even more (Tavassoli et al., 2024). Jiang et al. (2023b) revealed that FeCo-MOF grown in-situ on magnetic biochar could mitigate antibiotic risk by enhancing the removal of antibiotics such as tetracycline and chloramphenicol, in addition to achieving fruit preservation. When it comes to loading polyphenols, MOF performs excellently as well. CD-MOF assembled with γ-cyclodextrin (γ-CD) and alkali metal ions constitutes intrinsic pores and extrinsic caged pores to encapsulate anthocyanins through the coordination of ordered stacks of cations, and the retention rate decreased from 63.3 % to 55.7 %, which was dramatically higher than that of the control group (58.6–35.7 %), when the light condition was increased from 1 to 3 hours (Su et al., 2024). MOF may also restrict the passage of specific gases, which may be of particular significance for the application of intelligent packaging systems in special storage environments (Fig. 3 i).

CDs typically prepared by a simple hydrothermal method, are carbon-based spherical nanomaterials with particle sizes generally smaller than 10 nm (Liu et al., 2024). The surface of CDs contains a variety of functional groups, such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups, providing chemical versatility, tunable optical properties, and promising biocompatibility (Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023). CDs can be synthesized by both top-down and bottom-up pathways, where the top-down approach involves the decomposition of bulk carbon precursors down to the nanoscale, mainly using electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation, etc. (Li et al., 2023c; Reyes et al., 2016). The bottom-up approach, on the other hand, grows carbon dots from small carbon-containing molecules, which is procedurally more reliable and more in line with the concept of green chemistry (Iqbal et al., 2012). Hydrothermal methods, which immediately carbonize biomass-rich materials without the need for pre-drying treatments, are the most widely used ( Fig. 4 a). Converting food waste to CDs usually operates at temperatures between 100 and 200°C, requires low energy consumption, and does not necessitate expensive microwave control systems or laser systems, making it practical and offering the potential for large-scale manufacturing (Dong et al., 2023). Guo et al. (2024) reported a multifunctional CDs obtained by using Sophora japonica extract (95 % rutin) as a single precursor and prepared by a hydrothermal method, which were doped into gelatin film. The π-π conjugated structure endowed the CDs with UV broadband absorption properties, and the presence of a large number of hydroxyl groups at the edges ensured excellent antioxidant capacity. With the rise of CDs addition, the elongation at break increased from 220 % to 358 %, and the antimicrobial activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Bacillus subtilis, and Listeria monocytogenes increased by 55 %-95 %. It was virtually non-toxic to the L929 fibroblasts of mice. Light, especially ultraviolet light, affects the lipids, amino acids, natural pigments and other components in food, threatening food quality and nutrition (Tripathi et al., 2023). A banana paste made into nano-CDs significantly improved the UV-blocking properties of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films, the pure PVA film only had a tiny absorption peak around 200 nm. As the content of CDs increased, the absorption values of the film rose significantly in all ranges from 200 to 600 nm, while the transmittance in the UV region (200–400 nm) decreased significantly (Figs. 4 d and 4 e). Meanwhile, it has been demonstrated that nitrogen-doped CDs possess a higher content of amine and amide groups, thus carrying more positive charges and causing more damage to microbial cell membranes (Chen et al., 2023). Also, nitrogen-doped CDs were prepared using lemon peel and ethylenediamine, and the higher nitrogen-doped CDs provided better inhibition of Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus after 6 h of light treatment (Figs. 4 b and 4 c). Also using nitrogen-doped CDs, Lin et al. (2022) achieved both pork preservation and monitoring, with a positive correlation between the fluorescence intensity of the films when the content of total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) was increased from 6.68 mg/100 g to 17.53 mg/100 g (Fig. 4 f). As functional elements, CDs act as antioxidants (Guo et al., 2024), antimicrobials (Guo et al., 2024), UV-protectors (L. Zhao et al., 2022) and releasers of active factors (Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023). In addition to this, CDs allow the fabrication of diverse smart sensors.

Lactoferrin is an iron-binding glycoprotein, and among its attributes, antimicrobial has been a top priority (Wu et al., 2023). Lactoferrin inhibits bacterial growth by several mechanisms, such as competition for iron ions or binding to porins (Gruden & Poklar Ulrih, 2021). Meanwhile, its antifungal activity, antiviral activity, and antiparasitic ability have been reported (Fernandes & Carter, 2017; Krzyzowska et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2019). Bovine serum albumin and immunoglobulin also possess antimicrobial capacity, but are rarely utilized in film manufacturing. Rantamäki et al. (2019) loaded biologically active immunoglobulins (Igs) onto milk protein-based films, but the Igs were quickly and completely released, failing to achieve long-lasting antimicrobial activity. There are fewer examples of antimicrobial proteins applied in food preservation, and it is necessary to emphasize that tiny environmental stimuli may inactivate both proteins and their derivatives.

In most cases, enzymes are used as trigger switches for the release of functional elements, but their antimicrobial activity should not be overlooked (Min et al., 2022). Natural lysozyme is a small monomeric protein with proven stability, remaining active at temperatures up to 120°C (Zhang & Rhim, 2022). The application of lysozyme in applied coated packaging relies on the alternating sequential deposition of oppositely charged polyelectrolytes, where positively charged lysozyme can bind to negatively charged polysaccharides (e.g., pectin, sodium alginate, etc.) (Zhang et al., 2021). There are also means that can serve as an aid. Lysozyme activity increased by 256 % after immobilization by N-succinyl chitosan, the introduced moiety being located on the C2-NH2 of chitosan (Niu et al., 2020). In addition to its bacteriostatic activity, lysozyme itself may serve as a film material. When lysozyme and tris(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine solution (TECP) were introduced into a filament protein solution at ambient temperature, the disulfide bonds within the lysozyme molecule were rapidly reduced. Spontaneous formation of high-strength nanofilms at the air/solution interface with amyloid self-assembly (Miao et al., 2024). Lactoperoxidase is isolated from milk and denatured above 70°C. It is also a popular biopreservation enzyme (Aprodu et al., 2014)·H2O2 first oxidizes Fe3+ ions thereby generating complex I, which subsequently enters the halogenation cycle or peroxidation cycle ( Fig. 5 a). Chitosan coating in combination with lactoperoxidase extended the shelf life of trout fillets by at least 4 days while maintaining favorable organoleptic properties (Jasour et al., 2015). In addition, Ma et al. (2024) reported that the iron core of biogenic ferritin could act as a natural nano-enzyme to scavenge superoxide radicals, while ferritin in prokaryotes had a greater ability to remove superoxide than eukaryotes, and that the difference in catalytic ability was mainly due to structural changes resulting from variations in the ratio of iron to phosphate in the iron core. Inspired by such natural enzymes, artificial enzymes constructed using metal ions are also playing an increasingly vital role in food preservation (Liang et al., 2023). Meanwhile, a polymeric nano-enzyme based on nanocellulose could mimic well-characterized oxidase-like and adenosine triphosphate-like bisphosphatase activities to form a sustainable active coating (Huang et al., 2023a).

Peptides come from a wide variety of sources, the lactoferrin mentioned above has many derived peptide forms (Wu et al., 2023). Moreover, bacteriocins are peptides synthesized by specific bacteria during metabolism and usually exhibit antibacterial properties, nisin are typical examples. Lu et al. (2022) constructed antimicrobial hydrogels through the electrostatic attraction of nanocellulose and nisin, the hydrogels formed a well-fabricated network with high loading efficiency for peptides. When hydrogel served as a paper coating, paper strength was increased by 14.67 %. The release of lactostreptolysin was pH-regulated, with a slower release efficiency at neutral. Most bacteriocins only inhibit sensitive bacteria and remain largely harmless to beneficial microorganisms in the human microbiome (Liu et al., 2022). Furthermore, bioactive peptides produced by Lactobacillus plantarum incorporated into chitosan formed coating emulsions, and exhibited positive antifungal activity against Colletotrichum gloeosporioides and Botryodiplodia theobromae (Ranjith et al., 2022). Meanwhile, according to Li et al. (2023d), the addition of ε-polylysine (a well-known antimicrobial peptide) resulted in enhanced cross-linking of the film, which performed favorably in the storage of chilled pork. Simultaneously, compared with chemically synthesized antimicrobial agents, antimicrobial substances transiently found in the microbiota have unique advantages in terms of safety and efficacy, which may provide novel strategies for the elimination of disease-causing bacteria. The antimicrobial peptides themselves have complex assembly behaviors and diversified binding capabilities (Shabani et al., 2024; Torres Salazar et al., 2024).

Probiotics can directly or indirectly counteract harmful microorganisms, but the retention of their biological activity in different biopolymer matrices needs to be considered (Zhao, Li, et al., 2023). Lactococcus lactis inhibits several enzymes such as polyphenol oxidase (PPO), peroxidase (POD), and phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (PAL) and therefore decelerates physiological responses, but exhibits different antimicrobial activities in different substrates (Ma et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2022). When mixed into gellan gum, probiotic vigor is enhanced, however, the rise in polyphenol content may bring the opposite result (Yang et al., 2023). Also, probiotics generally destroy the reticulation of polymers and lead to a reduction in mechanical properties, polysaccharides always have better mechanical properties than proteins and lipids, and simultaneously provide nutrients for probiotics, so they are often preferred as a substrate (Ma et al., 2019). Plus, when inulin served as a prebiotic, probiotic activity could be boosted by 30–40 % (Zabihollahi et al., 2020). Mojaveri et al. (2020) further improved the viability of Bifidobacterium animalis Bb12 by electrostatic spinning with the addition of inulin, the samples also showed high melting point (>200°C). With the continuous development of nanotechnology, single-cell encapsulation technology is gradually being developed for the maintenance of probiotic viability. Back in 2014, Park et al. (2014) achieved controlled degradation by encapsulating a single Saccharomyces cerevisiae in metal-polyphenol nanoshells (Fig. 5 b). Fe3+ and TA could self-assemble at two immiscible interfaces to form a protective shell on the surface of individual microorganisms (Kim et al., 2017). Microbial growth curves revealed that Fe3+-TA shells may inhibit cell multiplication, but the final cell density attained was essentially the same (Fig. 5 c), suggesting that the potential effects of such modalities on probiotic activities need to be further explored in depth. Guo et al. (2014) summarized in detail the metal ions suitable for complexation with TA and tested 18 of them, which is equally applicable to other polyphenolic materials (Figs. 5 d and 5 e). In general, high valence metal ions are more effective, but Cu2+ has also been widely utilized in recent years (Wang et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2024). For refrigerated packages, the inclusion of sodium carboxymethylcellulose as a cryoprotectant may improve strain viability (Liu et al., 2024).

Phages are biologically antimicrobial by infecting microorganisms and lysing the host, several phage mixtures self-amplify at 37°C (Tomat et al., 2019). Currently, phage formulations approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) as Generally Recognized as Safe (GRAS) are being applied with ever-increasing frequency in edible packaging (García-Anaya et al., 2023). Several researches have been conducted to demonstrate that phage can be loaded in edible films or coatings with low loss ( Table 2). Phage encapsulated in polysaccharide or protein films have been widely reported, e.g., phage A511 incorporated into whey protein-purulan polysaccharide composite film could achieve long-lasting preservation for at least 60 days. However, the increase of phage concentration may raise the opacity of the film (Kamali et al., 2022). The availability of bacteria also greatly impacts the functionality of the phage components, once the host bacteria are anchored in the food web, physical contact between the two will be difficult, and factors such as water activity and the physical state of the substrate will also affect the mobility of the phage (García-Anaya et al., 2023). Therefore, the mass application of phage is still challenging and may even be ineffective, requiring thorough study of the behavioral characteristics of phage within the coating (Vonasek et al., 2018).

EOs are mainly terpenes and their oxygenated derivatives. Thyme, peppermint, clove, and lemongrass are among the favored sources of EOs (Perumal et al., 2022). EOs consist mainly of terpenes and of non-terpene hydrocarbons (a small fraction), consist of multiple isoprene units. Certain polyphenolic compounds overlap in both of these categories, so EOs tend to possess superior antioxidant activity (Rout et al., 2022). Thus, polyphenols are also direct contributors to the antimicrobial effect of EOs. The complexity of its composition makes it challenging to determine its exact antimicrobial mechanism, though it may not depend on just one. The lipophilic nature of EOs enables them to cross the biomembrane and associate with a variety of targets within the bacteria, and the main mechanisms of occurrence are summarized in Fig. 6 a. When chitosan-sodium alginate-amphoteric starch films were encapsulated with cinnamon EO, the films inhibited Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus by 36 % and 30 %, respectively, and degraded up to 70 % in soil after 28 days (He et al., 2021). EOs can be directly incorporated into the film, but may also be prepared into small labels and placed inside packages to be released as vapor. Porous materials such as starch are well suited for loading EOs (Campos-Requena et al., 2017). However, it is inappropriate to focus solely on the antimicrobial or antioxidant properties of EOs, which exhibit intense odors and very low flavor thresholds, and are extremely susceptible to altering the organoleptic properties of internal food products, need to be employed judiciously. Within certain limits, such impacts are acceptable. The sensory scores of mango did not seem to be significantly reduced when bergamot EO was added to the composite film at 9 % of the weight of the substrate (Chi et al., 2019).

Modified atmosphere packaging (MAP) has become one of the most commercially available methods of freshness packaging. Active MAP injects gases in advance or adds internal components to control the release of specific gases, while passive MAP relies more on the permeability of the system itself to a variety of gases (Rashvand et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023). The concentration of oxygen has the greatest impact on food quality and therefore receives the most attention, yet it is not economical to store oxygen in internal components and slowly release it. Opara et al. (2019) listed various recommended O2/CO2 concentrations for the storage of fresh-cut agricultural products (Fig. 6 f). Inspired by the leaf stomatal structure, porous microspheres composed of chitosan-poly(L-lactic acid) served as gas switches in a smart system that could regulate the permeability of O2, CO2, and H2O in a controlled manner (Fig. 6 c). In addition, chlorine dioxide (ClO2) is of widespread interest because of its strong oxidizing properties, which allow it to efficiently inactivate microorganisms while avoiding residual toxic by-products (Zhang et al., 2023). Wang et al. (2024) reported a PLA-based microcapsule that could achieve 55.75 % encapsulation of NaClO2, after blending with citric acid microcapsules, gaseous ClO2 was released in a high humidity environment, and the release rate was regulated by the temperature and the ratio of the two microcapsules. Virgin fruits stored with this design showed a 63 % and 34 % reduction in fruit decay and weight loss, respectively, compared to the control after 18 days of storage. Nitric oxide (NO) is likewise a highly effective antimicrobial gas, requiring only 10−12-10−9 mol of NO to reduce the synthesis of microbial second messengers, which in turn reduces the synthesis of macromolecular biopolymers and weakens the structure of biomembranes. When the concentration rises to 10−6 mol, it also causes lipid peroxidation or DNA damage, further realizing the antimicrobial effect (Tian et al., 2023). Lipophilic SO2 also possesses broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties and mammalian tolerance of it has been verified (Yu et al., 2023). In addition to this, various plant-derived volatile organic compounds (VOCs) may also be applied for the control of spoilage organisms or food-borne pathogens, where antimicrobial resistance is achieved through direct destruction, induction of immunity or participation in pathogen metabolism (Fig. 6 b), and therefore extend shelf life. The main components of VOCs partially overlap with EOs. In-package plasma also falls under the category of MAP, where gases are stimulated to decompose, producing free radicals, free electrons, dense neutral molecules, or other ions, with several long-lived reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (RONS) that can function for long periods of time. Zhou et al., 2022 have provided a detailed overview of its basic production process and the implications for quality, nutrition, as well as answering concerns about its safety.

SiO2 increases compatibility between film materials, resulting in a denser structure and enhanced UV protection (Hou et al., 2019). For example, 5 % SiO2 nanoparticles doped into a composite film consisted of gelatin and camellia oil, improved the mechanical properties and significantly enhanced the inhibition of lipid oxidation while maintaining the respiratory strength and enzyme activities of vital biological enzymes (Jiang et al., 2023a). Apart from this, SiO2 can load other functional elements, and the EOs mentioned above are frequently utilized as their core material (Surendhiran et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021). Yan et al. (2023) developed a mesoporous nanosilica loaded with oregano EO, which effectively prolonged the quality deterioration (weight loss, browning, and softening) that occurred after 12 d of storage in Agaricus bisporus. The synergistic effect of SiO2 and EO resulted in better retention of sensory attributes, which is essential for consumer acceptance. In another intriguing study, the researchers loaded thyme EO into different types of mesoporous SiO2, confirming that the release of EO was regulated (Cui et al., 2021). And they described the release behavior using the Peleg model (t/(Mt − M0) = K1 + K2t). Moreover, some novel and efficient synthesis methods have also been proposed. During the conventional synthesis of SiO2, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) is hydrolyzed by NH4OH. When SiO2 nanospheres were dispersed in the matrix solution, NH4OH was produced by re-polymerization between the silicate chains, and the ionic strength of the solution was increased (Fig. 6 d). However, by stirring the SiO2 nanospheres in HCl solution and then further purifying them, the dispersion in solution condensed to form H2O instead of NH4OH (Fig. 6 e), and the thickness of the electrical double layers (EDLs) increased (Fan et al., 2023). In this way, the electrostatic repulsion between the SiO2 nanoparticles became stronger and more uniformly distributed in the system, theoretically providing superior work efficiency. But even food-grade SiO2 nanoparticles may cause a certain degree of harm to the human body. SiO2 nanoparticles accumulated in the livers of pregnant mice and their fetuses, inducing inflammatory responses and metabolic disorders, as well as causing significant alterations in DNA methylation levels (Zhan et al., 2024).

The above functional elements may also be paired and function together. Koshy et al. (2022) utilized AgNP-anchored CDs and added them to soybean isolate protein films along with chitin nanowhiskers, which notably improved the mechanical strength and thermal stability of the films, the DPPH radical scavenging activity increased from 2.5 % to 25.7 %. Moreover, when the sample was digested using n-heptane as a simulant, no specific migration of AgNPs in it occurred, indicating that this film is safe and applicable. In addition, the antioxidant activity of polyphenol-ZnO nanoparticles could be elevated by 5–9 folds after incorporated into alcohol soluble protein/gelatin films. Cherry packaging experiments revealed that the peak ethylene production of the experimental group was nearly 50 % lower than that of the control group, offering excellent freshness retention. Furthermore, an iron-doped CDs-based nano-enzyme (Fe/N-CDs) platform with excellent peroxidase-like (POD) activity inhibited the vital activities of drug-resistant bacteria in the absence of antibiotics (Cui et al., 2023). Fe/N-CDs prevented cell membrane adhesion and thus inhibited biofilm formation of pulmonary pylori (Hafnia. alvei), a phenomenon that may be caused by negative charge interference on the platform surface. Complexation of quercetin and Cu2+ via a ligand bond automatically dissociated in an acidic environment, exhibited effective inhibition of biofilm formation against both gram-negative and gram-positive bacterial model strains (Cheng et al., 2024).

However, combined applications may also cause problems. For instance, proteins such as lysozyme may cause aggregation and surface changes in metal nanoparticles, who in turn inactivate the proteins. Therefore, Xiong et al. (2022) introduced polymers to modulate the nano-interface between AgNP and the enzyme, calculated the binding energies with five polymers using density functional theory (DFT), and finally chose branched polyethylenimine (BPEI). After 48 hours of incubation at 37°C, the composite (Ag/LBP) removed 62 % of the Escherichia coli biofilm, and the LIVE/DEAD staining results were almost completely red (Fig. 6 g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different response modalities of active packaging
ce:section.ce:para: The headspace inside a food package is often understood as a narrowly defined storage environment, corresponding to a broadly defined storage environment outside the package, both of which might be applied as triggers for an active packaging system. The factors affecting the storage environment are quite complex and varied, depending on the actual requirements, one or more environmental factors may be selected primarily as the response constraints of the system.

The triggering of a pH-responsive system typically depends on gas variations in the headspace microenvironment inside the package, accompanied by certain humidity requirements (Du et al., 2023). Specialty foods that may ooze during preservation may also be monitored (Liu et al., 2022). Such systems generally involve two mechanisms: (i) the existence of a sensitive chemical bond between the active substance and the polymer; (ii) the disruption of the equilibrium of polymers containing weak acids or bases when pH is altered (Hou et al., 2023). Which means, the release component is either loaded inside the film or can be the film itself. At the same time, numerous previous studies have shown that CO2 produced by respiration of fruits and vegetables during storage and acids secreted by microorganisms reduce the pH of the microenvironment inside the package. The TVB-N generated when seafood or meat is spoiled will increase the pH of the microenvironment, so it is classified into pH decrease response and pH increase response according to different food storage conditions (Du et al., 2023).

Release of pH decrease can depend on acid-sensitive chemical bonding, for example, chitosan contains primary amine groups that can reversibly link antimicrobial small molecules with aldehyde groups (Chabbi et al., 2020). Heras-Mozos et al. (2022a) constructed chitosan-citraldehyde or benzaldehyde structures ( Fig. 7 a), which remained stable in buffers of pH 7 and 4 and dissolved rapidly in buffer of pH 3 (15 min). The released aldehydes have promising inhibitory effects on two fungi, Penicillium expansum and Botrytis cinerea. Compared to other methods of loading active molecules, covalent binding immobilization is more in line with the concept of "click chemistry", which offers unique advantages in the controlled release of unstable and volatile antimicrobial substances. Acid-base sensitive nanoparticles represent another hot topic of present day. Loading of cinnamaldehyde in halloysite nanotubes followed by positively charged poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH) and negatively charged poly(styrene sulfonate) (PSS) attached to the surface of the nanotubes via Layer-by-Layer (LbL) assembly as pH-responsive gates was used to extend the shelf life of fresh wheat noodles (Li et al., 2022b). Polyphenol-metal networks spontaneously dissociate under acidic environment and release contents (Xu et al., 2022). Some precursor substances, e.g., NaClO2, can generate antimicrobial gases under acidic conditions which is also considered as a mechanism for pH decrease response (Wang et al., 2024).

The response mechanism for pH increase is particularly related to the protonation and deprotonation of the carboxyl groups in the polymer, whereby the polymer chains repel each other to create voids and thereby release the contents (Hu et al., 2021; Song et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2023). Hydrogels perform other tasks while realizing the release. For example, diacetylene spontaneously polymerized under UV irradiation to form polydiacetylene with conformation-dependent color-changing properties (Chen et al., 2022). When the pH is increased, a gel-to-sol phase transition occurred to achieve the release of internal loadings, while the color of the whole system changed from blue to purple, which could be visually observed by the human eye (Figs. 7 b and 7 c).

Additionally, some MOFs may accomplish a response to increased or decreased pH (Ding et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2022). Many pH-sensitive linkers are capable of releasing loadings in different pH ranges, both the dynamic environment and the cleavage efficiency need to be critically discussed during the application and selection process (Khan et al., 2023). The specific mechanisms of different pH-responsive release systems vary widely, and Table 3 lists them with different response modalities and the range of applicability.

The polar groups in the polymer are highly bonded and the polymerization energy is high. When water enters the polymer system and forms hydrogen bonds with polar groups or bonded water molecules, it disrupts the original ordered structure (Hou et al., 2023). Even free water fills the free volume to some extent, thus increasing the distance between molecular chains. According to the "free-volume" theoretical model, the larger the chain spacing, the more frequent the movement of polymer chains, which increases the release rate of the active ingredient (Fujita, 1961). Moisture-responsive systems can be microbeads, films, micelles, MOFs, etc., and these materials may shift from glassy to glassy transition zones or rubbery states as humidity increases. And water also ruptures the ordered structure of MOF crystals, with loadings varying in rate and amount of release at different humidity levels (Fig. 7 d). As a common food material, the controlled release of cyclodextrins is a typical example which is affected by hydrogen bonding. High moisture conditions lead to a weakening of the hydrogen bonding between the internal functional elements and the cyclodextrins, thus escaping from the internal cavities (Du et al., 2022). The moisture response may also be realized through chemical reactions, with NaClO2 as a precursor substance to release ClO2 requiring not only an acidic environment as mentioned above, but also a considerable level of moisture (Wang et al., 2024). Allyl isothiocyanates (AITC) is a volatile antimicrobial agent formed by enzymatic hydrolysis of black mustard glycosides in mustard seeds, which also requires high moisture conditions for the hydrolysis reaction to occur (Bahmid et al., 2021). Besides, the moisture response mechanism also maintains a constant state of humidity inside the system (Zhou et al., 2021).

Temperature is also a significant variable in the transportation and storage of food, where high temperature allows for the rapid multiplication of microorganisms, and ultimately food spoilage. Increased temperatures also lead to accelerated ethylene release from ripe fruits and vegetables, so temperature-responsive ethylene removal packaging can be designed purposefully. Such as, the release of the ethylene inhibitor 1-methylcyclopropene (1-MCP) could effectively improve the quality of fruits and vegetables during storage, but it may have some effects on traits e.g. the color of the epidermis (Guo et al., 2020; Jia et al., 2018). The thermo-responsive Poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAm) is a stimuli-responsive polymer with a lower critical-solution temperature (LCST) of about 32 °C. When the temperature exceeds LCST, the original hydrophilic curly conformation transforms into a hydrophobic globular conformation (Hosseini-Alvand & Khorasani, 2023). This LCST is compatible with most storage environments and is a favorable choice for controlled release. The LCST of the different materials all varied, e.g., the LCST of the PVA/curcumin-loaded micelles reported by Ju et al. (2021) was 41°C, and the LCST of the hydrogels reported by Werzer et al. (2019) was 29°C. Furthermore, N-vinylcaprolactam (NVCL) is also a popular thermosensitive monomer that exhibits hydrophobicity despite its hydrophilic polymer, with an LCST of about 34 °C. When carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) was served as an emulsifier, the hydrogel strength got enhanced. NVCL cannot be hydrolyzed easily compared to PNIPAm, and even if it is hydrolyzed, it does not generate harmful small molecules. At temperatures below LCST, this hydrogel will absorb external moisture. With the increase of hydrophilic groups, the maximum swelling rate of the hydrogel could reach 2056 % (Shang et al., 2023). The synthesis of thermoresponsive materials is relatively complicated and relies largely on the modification of materials such as PNIPAm or NVCL, where hexadecane can also be taken as a temperature-responsive nucleus (Figs. 7 e and 7 f). Hexadecane maintains a melting point of approximately 20°C, the polymer shell might delay the phase change process of hexadecane. The prepared microcapsules transformed into liquid state above 20°C, which accelerated the internal dissolution rate (Zhao et al., 2023). Currently there exist only few studies related to temperature responsive systems for food products, and further development is urgently needed.

Light may trigger a conformational change, such as the rotation of the N = N bond of azobenzene under UV light irradiation (Fig. 7 g), transforming it from the trans isomer to the cis isomer (Marturano et al., 2019). Moreover, stilbene (Fig. 7 h), spiropyran (Fig. 7 i), etc. possess similar properties. The second type of response is the generation of ROS, specifically single-linear oxygen (1O2), which can non-selectively and directly kill microorganisms. As an example, porphyrin MOF (PMOF) with high porosity and stable crystal structure is very conducive to the release and diffusion of ROS. MOF-545 belongs to PMOF, and when it was doped into a film, it could produce a large amount of 1O2 under white light irradiation (Zhao et al., 2023). Photodynamic sterilization is a novel non-thermal sterilization technique that is greatly promising for industrial applications. However, its safety needs to be ensured, so natural photosensitizers are more appropriate to employ. Among these, curcumin is a frequently occurring material. Nevertheless, curcumin exhibits poor water solubility, and the strategy of using organic solvents to aid in dissolution is not safe enough for food-related applications (Roy & Rhim, 2020). The involvement of cyclodextrins improves the water solubility of curcumin, and the complex of the two has also been utilized in yogurt or ice cream and deemed to be safe and reliable (Mangolim et al., 2014; Marcolino et al., 2011). The electrons on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles undergo a transition under UV stimulation, generating electron-hole pairs. The electrons (e-) or positively charged holes (h+) could catalyze the formation of a strong ROS, which could catalyze the formation of ethylene into CO2 and H2O (Du et al., 2022). TiO2 could also synergize with other metal oxides to accomplish continuous catalytic (Xie et al., 2022). The requirement for temperature response by utilizing light to increase the regional temperature is difficult to achieve, because of the high requirements for thermally sensitive materials (Du et al., 2023). Razavi et al. (2020) assembled PNIPAm using spiropyran as an initiator for the atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) method, and the polymer conformation could be altered by UV irradiation to thereby modulate the temperature responsiveness.

The triggers of enzyme response fall into bacterial and fungal sources, with bacteria secreting a variety of extracellular enzymes including phospholipases, lipases, proteases, hyaluronidases nucleases, and glycohydrolases, while fungal mycelium secretes pectinases, cellulases, and ligninases etc. (Dong et al., 2024). The design of the enzyme response system is determined by the nature of the food, for instance, spoilage of fruits and vegetables is usually caused by fungi, while spoilage of meat and soy products is dominated by bacteria. PLA nanofibers were used as thymol carriers and then coated with pectin as a response layer using electrostatic adsorption, which was very sensitive to a variety of microbial products, especially pectinase secreted by Aspergillus niger (Min et al., 2022). Stimulating the material with multiple enzymes results in more reactions and thus more microorganisms being inhibited. An Intelligent controlled release system for dual response of β-glucuronidase and serine protease, which responds to release cinnamaldehyde to reduce the Escherichia coli in beef by more than 99.89 % (Lin et al., 2022).

Certain materials may also be sensitive to specific molecules. Yu et al. (2023) efficiently loaded SO2 using polyamindoamine (PAMAM) and employed glutathione as a response trigger. The investigators did not observe significant cytotoxicity or hemolytic effects at effective antimicrobial concentrations. Moreover, the structure composed by combining carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS) and HKUST-1 can be destroyed by phosphate solutions of different concentrations (Huang et al., 2020a). Although these designs cannot be applied in most scenarios of food preservation, they are still inspirational.

Multiple responses are very diverse, may take the form of dual or even triple and above. Aytac et al. (2021) encapsulated the antimicrobial components in cyclodextrins, which generally weaken the hydrogen bonding between the cyclodextrins and internal hydrophobes when the relative humidity exceeds 85 %. The whole was added back into a variety of fibers, allowing for further enzyme response. o-Nitrobenzyl ester or phosphate derivatives are common photocleavable protecting groups, appropriate modifications on this basis allow the addition of pH-responsive functionality (Huang et al., 2016). The magnesium organic framework designed by Li et al. (2023b) featured a pH and NIR dual-response composite system, which upon stimulation by an acidic environment firstly undergone degradation of the coating, releasing Ca2+ and PO4 3-, followed by the release of Mg2+ and gallic acid from the MOF structure, which ions could enhance the mechanical strength of the film. What's more, a CMC-based microcapsule could even respond to temperature, pH, glutathione and enzymes simultaneously (Zhao et al., 2023). Specialized hydrogels possess double-locked structural domains that enable rapid degradation in response to both stimuli, but stimulation alone or reversing the order of stimulation won't induce degradation (Fig. 7 j).

The concept of sequential release is generally associated with drug delivery, which combines different response modalities to maximize the efficacy of the system (Zhang et al., 2023). There are two typical types of release models in sequential release systems, one of which is where drug A and drug B are released at different time points and normally B is encapsulated in nanoparticles to prevent premature release (Fig. 7 k). The other model is where drug A and drug B reach a plateau at different time points, and once A is the first to reach the plateau, its release rate is almost negligible while B continues to release (Fig. 7 l). Achieving this specific sequence in turn relies on three main mechanisms: (i) different physicochemical properties of the molecules that can lead to differences in diffusion rates; (ii) different affinities between the loaders and carriers; (iii) successive releases due to structural dissociation from the outside-in.

There may be a need for different functional elements to act at different stages of food preservation, making this design valuable. The antimicrobial properties of gelatin films could be enhanced by constructing bilayer nanoparticles loaded with tea polyphenol/trehalose, the researchers simultaneously evaluated the release behavior of the films for both teat polyphenol and trehalose, and found the presence of a two-stage release pattern (Zhang et al., 2024). Xie et al. (2023) first constructed an alginate microcapsule, and then coated the surface with a layer of polydopamine (PDA), which immobilized Ag on the surface of the microcapsule with the help of the adhesive property of PDA and the in-situ reduction of AgNPs. This system first realized long-lasting antimicrobial activity through the release of AgNPs, and when there was a large change in pH, the core encapsulated substance could be released, thus achieving the concept of sequential release. PDA not only made it possible to introduce other substances on the surface, but also further reinforced the original carrier.

As early as 2008, Dainelli et al. (2008) have presented the legal and safety challenges regarding active packaging. (EC) 1935/2004 served as the main reference in the early years, minor changes in the composition or organoleptic characteristics of the food were permitted, but the active packaging must not mislead the consumers and needed to be adequately and correctly labelled. In 2009, the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) Regulation (EC) No. 450/2009 allowed for the application of smart active packaging in food products, stating that the main objective is to improve the quality, safety and shelf life of food products. Laws and regulations on packaging materials and food additives already exist in EFSA, FDA, China and other institutions/countries. However, given the very variable forms of active packaging, it is difficult for the regulators to take them into account in a comprehensive manner. The European Union (EU)'s efforts in this regard have been relatively notable. For example, the European Chemicals Agency (ECHA) has proposed possible restrictions on the production of chemicals aimed at reducing the use of per- and poly-fluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) (Lim, 2023). Fluorosurfactants or fluoropolymers are frequently found in packaging materials, waterproof coatings, and other products. This inspired researchers to search for more natural, safe, and biodegradable novel biomaterials for the development of active preservation systems. Meanwhile, although some nanoparticles such as CDs and MOF materials added to the active packaging system exhibit huge prospects, they may possess a certain degree of biotoxicity and require further safety evaluation, migrating exogenous substances need to be discussed exhaustively (Szczepańska et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2024).

The international assessment of migration of hazards in food packaging should take a multi-level, systematic and professional response to ensure food safety and public health. Firstly, in terms of regulation and standard-setting, reference should be made to the guidance of organizations such as the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and the Committee on Food Science and Technology (CEN), which specify the migration limits (MIGs) for food contact materials, and to the latest scientific research results, which should be used to develop specific migration test standards, such as ISO 15161 and CEN/TS 14234. Risk assessment should follow the framework of the FAO and the EFSA, and employ a quantitative risk assessment methodology to evaluate the mechanisms of migration of potential toxins in different materials, the amount of migration, and the toxicological effects on human health. In the process, migration tests can be conducted using simulated food and storage conditions to reflect the risks under actual use scenarios. At the same time, manufacturers should follow Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP) and publicize material composition and migration assessment reports to facilitate risk assessment by regulatory agencies and consumers. A public information platform should be established to raise consumers' awareness of the potential risks of food contact materials.

Development of novel materials, structures and technologies has always been a hot topic. For instance, in industrialized production, crosslinkers may be applied separately from the polymer solution to form resilient protective coatings directly on food surfaces based on a liquid in-situ approach (Liu et al., 2024). By doping ferroelectric semiconductors into the films and adjusting the annealing and polarization parameters, flexible films possessing different charge gradients of electroactivity can be obtained, causing effective oxidative damage to bacterial membranes (Song et al., 2024). In addition to antimicrobials, responsive removal of moisture, oxygen, ethylene, etc. is also worth exploring (Yildirim et al., 2018). In the design of controlled release systems, non-covalent binding is difficult to immobilize antimicrobial agents for long periods of time. The binding approach represented by electrostatic interaction is very common in the construction of active packaging. Covalent interactions deserve more attention, such as the reversible attachment of chitosan to aldehydes with the aid of imine bonds (Heras-Mozos et al., 2022a), such idea is valuable for long-lasting antimicrobials with responsive release.

Previous active systems have been designed to target bacteria and fungi, but Covid-19 reminds us that major public health epidemics can also be caused by viruses. Novel active packaging systems should be able to kill and isolate viruses to a certain extent. Liu et al. (2024) reported an in-situ one-step synthesis of highly efficient antimicrobial and antiviral films, which were produced by heat curing at 140°C for 10 min. On the one hand, the addition of β-FeOOH resulted in a nearly 4 log reduction in the infectivity of the film against influenza A virus, and on the other hand, it mediated the catalytic effect of H2O2, and the film could be dissolved after a brief immersion in the H2O2 solution without producing any solid waste. During transportation, film may have to face potential threats from different external liquids. Therefore, features such as oil-water separation and unidirectional moisture evacuation are also of interest (Ling et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2024). Tao et al. (2024) combined dual cross-linking supramolecular silicone polymer (BPDI) with hydrophobic SiO2 nanoparticles by simple spraying to prepare self-healing and self-cleaning superhydrophobic coatings at different interfaces. Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) was chosen as a soft chain segment to provide fluidity. With the continuous advancement of molecular simulation technology, the responsive decomposition of some biopolymers can be simulated by computer (Samantaray et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2023). The form of active packaging may not be restricted to films, but may also be coatings, hydrogels, and so on.

Increasing attention is being paid to the development of active packaging, and numerous novel functional elements are being developed. Some of them are expected to integrate multiple functions, and indeed, active packaging should not be limited to antimicrobials, but the regulation of other microenvironmental factors e.g. pH, humidity, atmosphere, and light is also critical. Moreover, the mainstream response modalities of active systems are summarized in this review, with the concepts of multiple response and sequential release meriting extra attention from researchers. It is worth mentioning that the application of active packaging requires ensuring the consumer's right to be informed in order to prevent the occurrence of food fraud. Interestingly, specific functional elements such as polyphenols and CDs might serve to design information-storing patterns that help counter food fraud while being antimicrobial at the same time. Hopefully there will be more of these designs with the concept of "kill two birds with one stone" in the near future. In addition, although novel functional elements show promising potential for anti-microbial and preservation applications, research on their safety and potential impacts on ecosystems is still limited. To address these research limitations, future studies should focus on multivariate experiments to systematically assess the synergistic effects and release properties of different functional elements under multiple environmental conditions, especially under actual storage conditions. At the same time, in-depth safety studies are needed to assess the biocompatibility of novel functional elements and their long-term impact on the ecosystem to ensure the sustainability of the developed active packaging materials in terms of food safety and environmental protection. Exploring novel materials and processes to effectively integrate multiple functional elements into a single packaging system for more complex response mechanisms and dynamic release will help enhance the comprehensive performance of packaging. By focusing on the above research areas, scientists can more effectively advance the development of active packaging technologies to address the multiple challenges of food preservation, safety and environmental sustainability.
